critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT FOR TIME-TO-FAILURE PREDICTION IS INTERNALLY CONTRADICTORY. The label is time-to-next-failure (TTNF) — a quantity that is defined by future events relative to the current measurement. A random split will place observations from the same vehicle at different points in its failure lifecycle across train and test sets. A vehicle that fails in month 18 will have telemetry from months 1-17 in training and its failure event in the test set, giving the model full knowledge of failure precursor patterns for that specific vehicle. This is vehicle-identity leakage. Additionally, TTNF exhibits strong temporal autocorrelation by construction — two adjacent weeks of telemetry for the same vehicle have nearly identical TTNF labels minus one week. The claim that 'cross-sectional patterns' justify the random split is false; TTNF is an inherently time-indexed label.

2. RMSE AS PRIMARY METRIC IS POORLY MATCHED TO MAINTENANCE DECISION-MAKING. Fleet maintenance decisions are threshold-based: 'schedule maintenance if predicted TTNF < 7 days.' RMSE measures average squared deviation across all prediction magnitudes, penalizing errors at TTNF=30 days equally to errors at TTNF=2 days. A model that perfectly identifies which vehicles will fail in the next 3 days but has high RMSE on long-horizon predictions may be operationally superior to a model with low overall RMSE. The design should use precision/recall at clinically meaningful thresholds or calibration metrics, not RMSE as the sole primary metric.

3. LINEAR REGRESSION IS AN INADEQUATE BASELINE FOR VEHICLE TELEMETRY. The design compares the LSTM ensemble against 'a baseline linear regression.' For time-series predictive maintenance, the appropriate baselines include: persistence models (TTNF = time since last maintenance), survival models (Cox proportional hazards, Weibull), or simple threshold-rule models. Linear regression applied to raw telemetry with no temporal features is trivially weak. If the baseline has no access to temporal structure while the LSTM does, the comparison is not measuring architectural benefit — it is measuring the benefit of having temporal features at all.

4. SENSOR NOISE IMPUTATION APPLIED POST-SPLIT MAY INTRODUCE LABEL LEAKAGE. The design states imputation is 'fit solely on the training data.' For time-series sensor data with missing values, mean/median imputation fitted on training data may use the global mean of the sensor across all vehicles and time periods. If the test vehicle set has systematically different sensor behavior (newer vehicle generation with different sensor calibration), the imputation strategy introduces a mismatch. More critically, for TTNF labels derived from failure events: if a sensor reading is missing near a failure event and is imputed using a healthy-vehicle mean, the imputed value carries information about the failure proximity that is unrepresentative of true signal absence.
all_issues_raised: Random split of time-indexed TTNF labels enables vehicle-identity leakage and violates temporal structure
RMSE is misaligned with threshold-based maintenance decisions; precision at operationally relevant horizons is more appropriate
Linear regression baseline lacks temporal features, making the comparison about feature engineering rather than architecture
Sensor noise imputation may introduce label leakage near failure events when fit on training global statistics
Stratified random split on TTNF labels with temporal telemetry data causes temporal proximity leakage: train and test windows come from the same degradation trajectories
Experimental design tests cross-sectional patterns but hypothesis claims LSTM temporal modeling is the source of advantage: logical contradiction makes the result uninterpretable
RMSE on right-skewed TTNF distribution rewards mean-prediction; 'precision at operational thresholds' is undefined and cannot be evaluated as described
Linear regression is not a competitive baseline for predictive maintenance; survival analysis or gradient boosting on engineered features is the appropriate comparison
Imputation method for multivariate telemetry is unspecified; mean imputation would destroy cross-sensor covariance structure that LSTM is designed to exploit
Vehicle-level data leakage from random split on multi-observation telemetry — same vehicle in train and test
Linear regression is too weak a baseline for failure prediction; survival analysis models are the appropriate comparison
RMSE model selection criterion is misaligned with the operational binary maintenance decision
LSTM sensitivity to imputation artifacts not validated; imputed timesteps may generate spurious attention
Ensemble architecture is ambiguous; parameter count and capacity mismatch with baseline not disclosed
